Hey guys, JB is in the house. The wolf is in the hen house here for your weekly intensive training. And today's topic is one that I'm doing because of popular demand. It's time to talk about that big bear of a topic entrepreneurship, right? And here's my struggle. So when I decided to come up with this platform, I was torn between which direction should I go? Should I go straight on with sales and, you know, persuasion training, or should I make it really broad and focus on things like marketing and entrepreneurship? And here's the deal. And there's a lesson in this and it's kind of why I'm leading off with it is that on some level, we always want to be what people don't perceive us to be. Meaning my whole adult life, very early on, I developed the reputation for being the world's greatest or one of the world's greatest salespeople, right? And then very quickly after that, developed a reputation of being the world's greatest sales trainer. And that was especially true after I met at the straight line. And I use that to train, you know, thousands upon thousands of salespeople when I was very, very young, build a massive company, go absolutely hog fucking wild, right? Make a million dollars a week, make tons of other young kids rich, but I made a lot of mistakes along the way. But what came out of that was a system, the straight line. And when you have something that's just that awesome, like the straight line is, you can't kill it. It's an idea. Ideas can't be killed. So it reemerged from the ashes of my life. And lo and behold, here we are. When I went back out teaching the straight line 2009 and I went back in and I sort of redid the straight line and they purged out all of the stuff that I found to be objectionable. Like, you know, I was less careful when I was young, right? So now the version of the straight line piece today is completely ethical. It centers on ethics and integrity, right? And the language patterns are high pressure. All that stuff is gone replaced by more elegant and more effective strategies, right? And quickly when I went out there and started teaching sales, my business just soared. It's just soared sales, right? Also, on top of that, I have an equal understanding of what it takes to be an entrepreneur. And I pride myself on my ability to train and motivate young entrepreneurs to succeed massively. In fact, you give me any young entrepreneur who's just getting started. And I know I could coach them, mentor them to massive success. Maybe they won't be successful right away because that's not the way the game works, right? But ultimately you have me on your side and I'm showing you what to do and what not to do, you're going to succeed. So I was determined when I went out there into the marketplace to spread that message. Like, I teach sales and entrepreneurship. And I kept getting thrust into sales. So he's the greatest sales trader, the straight line. So I tried everything. I called it straight line entrepreneurship, you know, entrepreneurs boot camps, you know, even though and all the salesmen signed up and they all want to hear the straight line, sales, sales, sales, sales. Now, in truth, of course, sales is one of the most, well, it is the most important skill bar none in achieving success, whether you're an entrepreneur or not, you just have to learn to influence and persuade. But the point was is that I had this like this anchor around my neck that like, you know, I'm just being recognized for my ability to train salesmen. I also can train entrepreneurs and I was determined to do that. So when I launched this new platform, my first instinct was to, you know what, I'm going to make it about sales, entrepreneurship, marketing, and then my ego thankfully got checked by my age and 56 years old now. So I'm a bit smarter and wiser and I was able to check my own urges to essentially prove to people that I'm more than just a great sales trainer. And I said, you know what, the, if I want to really ensure my own success and hit it out of the park, let me give people what they want, give them what they expect from me. People want me to train them to be salespeople. They want me to teach them the straight line. Why? Because it's fucking awesome, right? And as we all know, it's now it's history that the idea worked out. The platform took off like a rocket and here we are now on the verge of making history and redesigning training online into this new model where you pick a very low price and there's no selling going on and just purity of message and it's fucking awesome, right? Good for us. That being said, entrepreneurship is an integral part of being in sales because when you are a salesman, the great ones all can fit themselves being in their own business because you have to think like a business owner. So on the platform, I go through all the messages pretty much every day or so. I look through to make sure, see what, see what the vibe is. And I've seen a lot of people requesting training on entrepreneurship, right? So I said, you know what? Okay, fair enough. I'm going to give the people what they want, but I want you to understand here that whether you're in sales or you're an entrepreneur, this training is for you. If you're in sales, you have to think like a business owner. So I'm going to be teaching entrepreneurship today in a way that applies to everyone, whether you're in business or not. So my take on entrepreneurship is pretty much a philosophy on life. It's a philosophy on how you go about just achieving anything you want. Now there's different angles to this because remember, there's always like the inner game and the outer game. So watch what we have in straight, and this is really a straight line entrepreneurship. And remember what the straight line really is when I talk about straight line sales or straight line entrepreneurship or straight line venture capital. What I'm really saying is that it's a backwards looking system. So rather than trying to just go forward and close someone, you step out and imagine yourself at the end point saying, okay, in order to close someone, what had to line up? I look back, I look forward, right? So I'm at the end of the sale and I go backwards. What are the elements that have to line up, right? Now in straight line selling, we have the inner world of success and the outer world of success, right? Well, the same thing goes in entrepreneurship. Now the main difference is that there are certain key things about being an entrepreneur or you'll be a commission based salesman that you simply are required to know. The certain counterintuitive moves that you must make in all aspects of your life to really succeed at the highest level. So when I speak of straight line entrepreneurship, I divide it into two categories. You've probably heard me say this before, but I'm going to dig much deeper now and give you my absolute best stuff right here. And then I will answer your questions afterwards, not right now, guys, because you know, you're seeing this tape because right now I'm a fucking high on morphine and I'm still recovering from my having my joint replaced, right? But as soon as I recover, we're going to do a Q&A on this topic here. So save your questions and think about them carefully here, because this is going to be really an awesome training. I know everyone's going to love this training. So when I think of straight line entrepreneurship, it's the intersection of a bunch of different things. For instance, a certain type of goal setting and vision, the integration of vision and goals, because as an entrepreneur, you must be a visionary. You have to be a visionary to be an entrepreneur, because here's the deal. Being an entrepreneur is about leading people. It's about taking an idea and bringing that idea to market and delivering value to large numbers of people and to make money by doing that. Now, in order to deliver value to many, many people, you can't go it alone. You're not a one person army. So there are two, this is like, okay, there's like three separate equations I'm going to go through today that are the initial equations for entrepreneurship. All right, they're independent from each other, but they all matter. The first one I'm going to talk about is the way I divide up the skills of entrepreneurship with the two chunks on one side. So here's entrepreneurship at the top, right? And on one side of the equation, as an entrepreneur, you must learn how to do what I call fail elegantly. Now, when I first came up with this, at first, my utter those words was probably like in nine, it was in 2009, when I was kind of first thinking about this. And when I first said that everyone was like, wow, that's really profound. Like I never really had heard that before. And since then, of course, everyone has copied it. So I don't think it's probably, no, I probably no one gave me credit for it. But this was something I came up with very early on in the game. Because I was saying to myself, you know, I look back at my own life and said, well, the first time I really went into business, what went wrong? Well, I started a meat and seafood company from scratch after getting out of dropping out of dental school, right? And basically I broke all the rules of entrepreneurship. And rather than failing elegantly, which means failing, what does it mean? Number one means failing quickly. It means failing cheaply without losing that much money. And it also means failing in a way that maximizes on the lessons that you learn. In other words, when you fail, and people say, oh, don't say fail. Failure is a bad word. No, it's not. Failure is part of life. Okay, or failure is just simply, like as Thomas Edison once said, he, you know, I just figured out another way not to invent the electric light bulb, right? Took him like a thousand tries. So every time he failed, that's one more way not to invent the light bulb, right? The point is, is that when you are failing elegantly, you're acting, taking actions and doing things in a very deliberate way that allows you to minimize on how much money you lose, how much time you waste failing, because time is money, and also puts you in the appropriate mindset to look objectively at what happened and maximize on the lessons of your failure. Because everyone who's ever succeeded, they've also failed. And that will tell you, experience will tell you that it's in your failures that you learn. That's where you learn. That's where you grow. It's where you develop muscle, you get tougher, but also for you simply by living the lessons of failure, you just simply grow as a human being and get stronger, right? So that's one side of the equation is learning how to fail elegantly. And there are five core distinctions that I teach people that are really easy to follow. Yet for some reason, if you don't teach this to people, they tend to do the exact opposite. I call that being counterintuitive. In other words, it's almost the opposite of what you might think you should do. If you were just being a lot, if you were trying to logic out how to start something, you would almost do the opposite because you would think, you know, that would be the smarter way. But in reality, because it's counterintuitive, it turns out to be the worst thing you could do. So we have five tactics that I want to go through today that are easy. They're simple to learn and they are steadfast, meaning you cannot break these rules. In fact, what happens when you break these rules is essentially like you're tempting the god of fate, right? The fake god is a motherfucker, by the way. And when the, if you tempt the fake god, he will piss on you. Okay. And by the way, his urine stream is like a fucking fire hose. He doesn't fuck around this guy. So when you tempt the fake god and break these rules of failing elegantly, you're done. You're going to feel massive pain. Now, if you're lucky, if you're like me, by the way, you can overcome and you can put up with the abuse of it all, you will pick yourself back up, right? But I can tell you that I wish that the 50 year old Jordan could have whispered in the 21 year old Jordan's ear saying, Hey, buddy, listen, these are some really basic simple things that you need to do when you're breaking all these rules. And there's simply no way you can succeed with the way you're doing things. Because very, very rarely does your first iteration of an idea actually work. It takes a few pivots and nips and talks and changing directions for you to actually get what you want to get the results, right? That's another formula I'll talk about later. So we must learn how to fail elegantly. And then on the flip side of the equation, what do you do when you go into business and you abide by the strategies of failing elegantly, which allows you to test an idea, test the concept in a way that if you're wrong, you fail quickly, cheaply and maximize on the lessons. But what about when you're right? What about when your test works and the results that you get exceed your expectations? Like for example, I started this platform, you guys have watched me abide by these rules in lockstep. I have followed these rules in lockstep, which is why we were succeeded together here with this platform. In other words, there was a certain point when I looked at my staff and said, guys, we've arrived. This shit is right. It's exceeding my expectations. It's going to be huge. And all the tactics, they fall, they flip on their head. And you go into a different set of strategies called succeeding wildly. And what that means essentially is what do you do? How do you act? What moves do you make when the shit is right? When you test an idea and every once in a while, you get that amazingly stupendous result that shocks even you and you're like, mother fuck, I got a scale. How do you scale? How do you take a small business and turn it into a large business? How do you take a large business or a medium-sized business and turn it into a multinational corporation? How do you prepare yourself to eventually either get bought by another company, take yourself public, raise money from venture capital? How do you do all those things? Well, those skill sets fall into this side of the equation, meaning how do you then grow like a weed? How do you control that growth? How do you scale and set yourself to be sold or go public? Because remember, at the end of the rainbow is always going to be some sort of exit ramp. We go into business either to a, one day sell the business, be retire from the business. See a great business allows you to walk away from the business. I'm building this business right now and it's relying on me. I'm aware that right now I carry the brand on my back. I'm essentially the talent, so to speak. I do the training, I do the intensives, I do the daily motivations, I run the company behind the scenes that make decisions. I have a huge staff, I'm building in the process, but right now it's very much dependent on me. But let me ask you a question. And honestly, as much as we enjoyed this, I know I'm good at what I do. I'm not trying to brag. I'm just being honest here. Is that a sustainable business model? Think about it. The perfect example is this training right now. So I had to tape this training in advance. I'm going to be in the hospital recovering from a pretty severe surgery. So fortunately, the surgery is, I have like a four week recovery, so I can tape it and sort of fudge it by delaying the training one week so I can still do the Q and A's. And there won't be much of a hiccup. I'll tape the daily motivations in advance. But imagine if I really got sick and it had to be for three months, and imagine if I had no warning. What then? Well, I think that I've built up enough equity with you guys and trust and respect that you wouldn't abandon the platform if I got sick. My team would repurpose content for a while and you would hang in there and you'd do it. You would hang in there a long time until it just, you know, you realize that shit, it's over. The dream is over. He's gone and there's nothing better place it, right? So is that really a sustainable business model? Think about that. Think about in your own life right now, whatever it is you're doing, you know, is it all about you? That's not a business. That's not a real business. It's a one man army, a one person army. I always say sometimes I feel like I'm the guy, the one man dead. I'm hitting the fucking bass and playing the fucking cymbals. I'm playing the piano with that. I'm singing, right? That's how I felt especially in the beginning. Now less so. But remember now, what's my vision for this company? My vision is to start bringing other amazing trainers onto this platform and using these monthly subscriptions rather than just trying to pocket it all. Yeah, that will make me the most money in the short term. That'll be very greedy and short-sighted rather than just pocket the money. I reinvested in more advertisers to grow the platform even more. And now I'm going to start spending money, a lot of it each month to hire the best trainers in the world and pay them so they can afford to come and teach you without having to sell you shit because the two can't happen at the same time. As soon as they try to sell you shit, they stop teaching you. They can't, they don't happen well together. So in other words, what I'm trying to say here is that my vision for the future, what I'm building towards has to be bigger than me. And you have to feel the same way about whatever your business is because if your business is just reliant on you, it's not sustainable for the long term. It's not really a business. It's a mom and pop operation. Now all great businesses or 99.9% start off as those mom and pop style operations. The biggest companies in the world start out of someone's garage or someone's basement like my business just did, right? Well, when I was very young, out of a backyard. In other words, great businesses start that way, but there's got to be a vision that guides your decisions. So while you have these two equations that I'm going to get specific about these today, I'm going to do this training. I'm not going to do it all in one weekend. I'm going to do it like over probably three weeks. I'll spread it out a little bit, do this and go back to sales. So I'm just going to start peeling back delays of how you build and grow a business and make tons of money and duplicate yourself, right? So while this is your overarching equation, now let's talk about the second equation for being an entrepreneur. There's three things that you must know how to do to really succeed wildly as a businessman. Number one is you must develop this big V, the fully integrated vision for your future. Now, as a salesperson, for daily motivation, right, to motivate yourself to help you break through the barriers and obstacles and negativity of being a salesperson, the rejection, you need to have a vision. And I've already been through that and explained to you how your vision sits atop your goals, right? And then we have our goals here, here, here, here, short term, intermediate, long term that connect to your vision and your goals are there to simply tell you whether you're right or whether you're wrong when you're heading in the right direction or wrong direction. So you set a goal, right? You put a date on it and then when you take action based on the results you get, you see am I heading in the right direction or the wrong? So the sum of all those results, essentially, and the pivots that you make based on those results, right, will ultimately push you towards your vision, right? That's what your vision for the future is about. So while as a salesperson, the vision is about essentially keeping yourself motivated and understanding your why so you can break through the barriers and obstacles as a business owner from an entrepreneur's perspective, the vision is much deeper and more profound because the vision is what allows you to attract other people to come work for you and help turn your vision, your business into a reality. Remember, I've said this before, every human being is thirsty for a vision, everyone, and so few human beings have one. So because they lack a vision, what do they do? They'll gravitate towards someone who has a compelling vision. You get it? Because most people don't have a vision. They all want, they want to be part, they want to feel that, they want to feel that inspiration. Remember the movie Jerry Maguire, what does she say? You know, and he, anyway, is standing in the elevator or something, and he goes, you know, why did you leave the job? You're a single mom, you don't have healthcare, she goes, you know, why did you come with me? And she goes, I just want to be inspired. That's not a word, very profound. I just want to be inspired. When you have a compelling vision for your, like a mission statement, a vision for your company, they go very much together, right? What it allows you to do is attract really talented people who will actually work for far less money than if they were just working based on a logical reason, like simply I'm a hired guy, I have no attachment to the company. When someone has an emotional attachment, which is on this level of vision, you can hire the best people and they will work their asses off to help you turn it into reality. Now, the counterpart to that is you need to be fair. So if you want to sell your vision to other people, then you also be, have to be willing to spread the wealth. So like in my company, I have every intention of making sure that everyone comes along for the right. And I've always done that. That's why you sort of strattened everyone made money. I could have changed the commission structure, people would have worked for far less, but that wasn't what it was about. It's all for one one for all to sell a vision for the future. Now, unfortunately, that vision was bankrupt and it was based on greed and a lot of things that really weren't right. It didn't start off that way, but it evolved that way. Now older, wiser me, I have this incredibly pure vision and every person at my company would kill for the company. They want this to happen because it makes sense. It's right. And they're emotionally connected. Yes, they'll all get rich from that, but you must have this vision. You have to have that. Without it, you will never grow past being an average company. People will come and go. You'll have all gunslingers hired people that come and go the next highest bidder and you won't have something special that people love and connect to. You get it? That's what vision means on the level of entrepreneurship. Now, remember a vision also must be malleable. A vision is a living, breathing organism that changes and grows. And the way you do that, this is where this is where some of the lessons that we've already learned with sales really come home to roost so to speak is that this is when you really must harness that power of what I call bifurcated focus. Bifurcated focus means that you create this vision, this bold, bright, compelling vision for the future and you lock onto this with a laser guided focus with one eye, one eye, your focused laser light, but the other eye is constantly scanning the horizon for other opportunities. And when you see one that makes sense, you integrate that into your vision and your vision of vows and grows. Guys, my vision for this company as it is today, it didn't start off this way. You get it? It started off with like my original vision was, I think there's a bankrupt culture out there that's gotten more and more corrupted of people giving these free webinars where they waste your time and teach you nothing and bait and switch you. I have a solution to that. It would be paid trainings where you give massive value and so forth. That was my original vision. Well, from there, it started to grow and evolve to bring other people on our social platform. It was just so many little pivots and opportunities I saw because I'm never focused on that. It was called tunnel vision. Tunnel vision is just simply saying that's my vision. That's my vision. I can't look at anything else because it's not in my box. There is no box. You must always think outside the box, but that doesn't mean going off half cocked with every little idea. No. So one of my locks on the vision and the other eye stands for opportunities that can be integrated. It's not separate. It's integrated into the vision and makes the vision more powerful, more compelling, and also a better business model fundamentally more sustainable. So that's essentially this first aspect. Number one, you must have a vision for the future. Number two, you must have the ability to share this vision, to share the vision with people in a way that moves them to take action and make your vision a reality, to essentially come work for you. In other words, you could have the most compelling vision for the future, but if you can't share that vision with other people, if you can't verbalize that and explain it in a way where other people get excited about it, bam, I want to be a part of that, good luck trying to recruit people. Like I said, you're never going to be a one person army. As a visionary, as an entrepreneur, we must bring other people in that possess the skill sets that lack, that allow us to leverage our own talents even more people that we can trust to watch the store when we're not there so we can grow and grow and grow. You get it? So you must and by the way, of course, the beauty is, is this is the core of what we do here. This is sales, persuasion, influence, storytelling. That's what sharing your vision is. This is why I say, I say, you know, I said sales is everything in life. You're selling, you're failing, you're selling people that your ideas make sense, your concepts make sense, that your vision for the future makes sense. That's what I talk, I'm talking about. You're selling your kids to make their beds, do their homework, you're selling your employees on the value of working for you, making your vision a reality. You're selling venture catalysts on investing in you so you can have the cash show to grow and turn into even bigger, blah, blah, blah. You get it? You must learn how to share the vision. The beauty is for this, we have the straight line. So this, well, let's just check this box off here because by the time you're on this platform for a while, you'll have no problem with that. Okay? You just won't because that's what we specialize in. And then the third and final aspect of this is that you must have the vision, the ability to share that vision, and also you must have the actual real world business strategies. And guess what happens? Watch, which takes us back to this, the real world business strategies to turn your vision into a reality. Actual strategies, what are the rules of entrepreneurship? What are the there are things you must do, must things you must not do, the certain processes you must be proficient at. And if you lack them, I promise you, no matter how good your idea is, you will fuck it up. Plain and simple. Businesses are about people. Show me an average business idea, like iffy with great people and they'll make it work. They'll pivot, they'll do something, change it up and boom, they'll grow. Show me the best idea in the world run by a bunch of idiots. And I promise you will end up in the crap and it will fuck it up 100% of time. You must have certain types of systems and certain talent pools in a company. My favorite quote in this department is someone I have tremendous respect for Warren Buffett. Warren Buffett said that when he buys a company, when he looks at it, he wants to make sure that the operations, the systems in place are so simple, so easy to follow, so foolproof, foolproof that even a moron could do them. Because sooner or later, one will get it. You can't avoid the moron factor in life. The world is full of them. You're going to, as hard as you try, try as you might, you're going to be hiring people who are not that smart. And then also, of course, there is the Peter principle where people rise up to a level of their own incompetence. I'll get to that later because it's a huge danger. But Warren Buffett's quote is so true that the systems that you have in a business must be so ABC simple to follow that even an idiot could follow them because sooner or later, one will be following them. You're going to hire an idiot and unless that system is in place, this person is going to just fuck it all up. So that being said now, let me move on to the third and final overarching formula before we get specific. And that is the most, and I actually went through this formula. I touched on this in an earlier training in a different context in the context of goal setting. And I spoke about the basic entrepreneurs formula. Guys, I did not invent this. In fact, I don't think anyone did. It's just, it's fucking been around forever. It's pure logic, but some people would like you to think they made this themselves and it's a crock of shit. Okay. So I don't take credit for this, although maybe I can teach you in a way that allows you to apply it in a far more effective manner. So it's the very basic entrepreneurs law of a know your outcome. Know your outcome. Step two, take massive action. Step three, analyze your results. And step four, change your plan accordingly. Change your plan. This is the oldest the fucking sees, right? All right. Now, the problem is that you can't follow this. Just when someone teaches this to you in a seminar and says, here's as simple as this, it doesn't work. There's so much more with this that's going on that. And I, when I went through that in the last lesson, actually, I'll find out which one it was and I'll put a footnote on this training where I really dig into this formula here in terms of how you go about, you know, setting a goal and achieving and knowing your outcome, right? Because what happens is there's, you know, questions that obviously arise here, like know your outcome, take massive action. Oh, really? With what plan? How do you develop the plan? Who do you need to know? What groups, what people, what skills need to be, come on, you know, these four things. So if you were in a seminar and some guy gets up and starts screaming, take massive action and change your plan accordingly, how many times as many as it takes, just know that it's just a worthless exercise you're going through. Because that into itself, while it's all true, it's not applicable. You can't use it without knowing a heck of a lot more. So I dug into this separately in a different training. I'll do it again another time, but I want to move forward here and get to this back to the core of today about failing elegantly and succeeding while. But I want to understand that this is sort of that last formula, okay, of knowing your outcome, taking massive action using the best plan available, identifying people, groups, organizations, and then when you take massive action, you're analyzing results. Of course, most of the time, you're not getting the result you want because that's life as you're figuring shit out. And this is when your state management and modeling and all these different things I spoke about in another lesson, I want to get, I need to speak to this for three hours, by the way, okay. And then this idea of analyzing and so forth, pivoting and running this loop as many times as possible, okay. That's your third formula that really governs entrepreneurship, right. So I'll get back to this in another training one more time from a very entrepreneur centric position versus what I did last time, was what's sales centric, right. But for this training, I want to focus now on this. Right here. Okay. The idea of failing elegantly and succeeding while. So here's the deal. Let me tell you the story. When I went into the meat business for the first time, okay, this is my, when I was 21 years old, right. So I go to, I graduated college and I didn't know what I wanted to do for a living, right. Because you know, I was 21. If you ask me, you know, Jordan, what do you want to be for a living? I want to be rich. I didn't know what I wanted to like most kids, right. Now my mom wanted me to be a doctor. I didn't want to go to medical school, right. Because it was eight more years. My uncle was a dentist. He was very wealthy, right. My mom wanted me to know a doctor dentist. I'd still be doctor Belfort, right. And my mother, since I was like two years old sitting in the high chair from the time she was spoon feeding me applesauce, she'd say the only noble way to be wealthy, Jordan, is you got to be a doctor, a dentist as the apple sauce went in. Docs like fucking hypnosis, doctor, right. And so that, and that's a belief that my mother inserted in me and beliefs are powerful. So when I was 21, graduating, you know, what I wanted to do is say, well, you know, I can't be a doctor because that's eight, 10 more years. I'll kill myself. I'll be a dentist. I'll still be doctor Belfort. My mother will be happy. Dentists get rich. And that was that. So I applied to dental school as a very good student. I got in, right. My first day in dental school, the dean stands up and you might have heard this story before. I'll tell it quickly. And he says, welcome to the Baltimore College of Dental Surgery. You should be proud to be here. Dentistry is a wonderful profession. Blah, blah, blah. You're a pillar of their community. And it's me, about a hundred other kids in the audience that we're all saying, all right, so far, so good. The dean had this white jacket on, very dental looking with white like so far, so good, right. And then the dean says, but let me say this, the golden age of dentistry is over. If you're here to make a lot of money, you're probably in the wrong place. I'm like, what the fuck? I'm in the wrong place. And I got up and I fucking bolted. I left the first day. You should have seen the looks on the other kids faces. They were dying, right? Never went back. Now, I couldn't tell my mother this. So I hid down in Maryland for a few months until my money ran out from selling ices on the beach at the summer before, right? And I finally had to get real with my life. And I answered a blind and in the paper in New York, we'll move back to New York, move back and my parents don't picnic. I'm going to show you, right? I answered this blind and it was for selling meat and seafood door to door, right? And to shorten the story here, I trained for one day and then my first day on the job, I shattered the company record for sales and never looked back, never looked back. And within the first week, I was so, I was like 400% above the biggest salesman there. Everyone was looking to be for direction. And since I prided myself on being an entrepreneur, because I'd been entrepreneurial since I was very young, I had a paper, I did magic shows, I made money selling ices on the beach. I know that story, right? So I was like, why should I work for these bozos? I said, I'll just buy my own truck. I'll go down to the meat market, get the meat, I'll get a warehouse, blah, blah, blah. And I launched my own business and I bought a truck and I started selling. And I made a lot of money. So it was a great salesman. So I bought a second truck, trained someone. Whoa, I'm great at training salesman. So I bought a third truck and then a fourth truck. And before I knew it, I had 26 trucks on the road. I was 22 years old now, just starting 22. I was training salesman every morning, running this warehouse with meat and a freezer, everything, making a ton of money. I thought, but actually I was making every mistake that a young entrepreneur could make. I was over expanding, I was growing on credit. And just like that, when before I knew it, the barbecue season ended, it was New York, right? So the season during the summer, it got cold, people reduced the amount of meat they were buying, my payables came due and boom, the business collapsed on me and bam, I was out of business and bankrupt at 23 and lost everything, lost it all went out of business. Why? Well, in truth, that was a terrible business model. It was a shit business to start. But even so, I broke every rule there was to starting up a business. I broke the rules of entrepreneurship. And because of that, because I broke the rules, I essentially created a snowball avalanche of shit that I had to deal with and it ended up driving me into bankruptcy. So let's get specific now. Okay, I have a list here I've made for you guys of what are the actual rules of entrepreneurship. If my computer will turn on, there it goes. Okay, so here they are. I want to, because I don't want to run out of time. So let's go very quickly here and go through the actual rules of entrepreneurship. Essentially, failing elegantly means operating in a way, making your decisions in a way that allows you to conserve cash, test your ideas, limit your losses and do it quickly. Okay, so let's go to number one. The first thing is called keeping low startup costs. In other words, when you go into a business, when you have an idea, and you want to turn that idea into an actual business, there's going to be certain things that you need. You might need that rent space, you might need to buy a vehicle, you might need to, you know, buy certain types of software, you might have to need certain types of hardware, right? You might have some travel expenses. There's just going to be expenses, right? And there's always going to be two routes to go. Two routes to go. You're going to have one which is going to give you high startup costs and one route is going to be low startup costs. I want you religiously with a religious fervor here to focus on low startup costs, meaning anything you can do you engage in what's called bootstrapping. You bootstrap. It's a separate set of lessons I'll teach you another time here. Bootstrapping means that you do things on the fly, give you a perfect example. When I started this business, right, I did not rent space. I had a little room, like my cellar, it might weren't my home that was basically broken. Now he had let it go. He never like to put money into it and it was a disgusting place, right? But rather than going out there and renting an office or anywhere, anything, I said, you know what? If I keep it here, it costs me nothing. I can test the idea like that. Another example, when I was getting started, right? I decide I use my old, old laptops. I didn't even, I needed, my people wanted me to get a super duper $5,000 Apple computer that did graphics and all this stuff that's my, my editors wanted. Absolutely not. I wouldn't do it. Now, of course, now we all have the best computers now, right? But in the beginning, while I was testing my idea, I'm sorry, I know it's going to take me an extra three hours to do this, to download this and also to whatever it's called, the process when you have, what's it called, Siri, and when they, when something is rendering, that's the word, right? When something's rendering, here we go, here we go. So it's rendering, right? On a super computer, it renders quickly on a regular laptop, it renders slowly. You know what? Let it render slowly, okay? Because $5,000, even though I have money, I can afford it. That's not the point. It's a way of thinking. I'm not spending a fucking dollar on a pen I don't need. When I'm starting the business, I have a reputation for living big in my own life. That's true. I'm not cheap. I spend money. I'm loose with money. But when it comes to running a business, especially in that startup phase, boy, I am doing everything I can. Anything that I can buy or lease, I will lease. I don't care if it costs me twice as much in the end. I'll pay double to conserve in it because here's what's the worst that can happen. Think of it this way. Any decision, you say, okay, I have two spaces I can rent. There's a space that's 1200 square feet. That will cost me $1,000 a month. But the problem is is that that's only going to last me for four or five months. As soon as I start to grow and succeed, I'm going to have to move and I'll be stuck paying at least for a year while there's another space that's 3,500 feet to $3,000 a month, right? And I have to put another couple thousand into it to get it bigger and more equipment, right? But that will take me for two years. I'd be a fool to go to the smaller space because I can only be there for three or four months. No. I'll gladly let me go to the small space, grow out of it, because if I grow out of it, it means what? I did what? I succeeded. Then I don't give up. I'll pay the F. So I'll move out. I'll pay the fucking empty space for six months extra. I'll find something to do with it. But the point is I make my decisions always early on the side of conserving my startup cash. I don't want to spend money on everything because at the end of the day, there's a statement that we you've probably heard before and I can promise it. It's true, especially in the startup phrase. It's called cash is king. You get it? Conserve cash. So whenever possible, any decision that you make, they say you can buy here's the trap. You can buy 2000 of these and it will cost you 50 cents a piece. But you could buy 50,000 of them at a nickel a piece. And I don't know. Don't worry about the math. The point is, is that the cost per unit is dramatically lower if I make a big order. I have to spend three times as much money so I can spend $500 or 2000 vowels. But if I spend 2000 on it, I have it for a year. So I'm getting a much better deal. I'll spend the 500 and pay extra. I don't want to hold inventory. I don't want to I want to conserve my money, my cash, all of because at the end of the day, that is what keeps time on my side. Now along with that, okay, so low startup costs is this mantra. I want you to live by this and die by it. Start out of your house. Fold your own ship, recruit your wife or your little brother or your mother or your father, whatever you have to do to keep your startup costs. Well, use your own car. Okay, use WhatsApp or Skype. Don't run up bills against your phone lines and ultra high speed to keep everything bare bones. That's your mantra. Number two, goes along with that is low overhead. So in the same way you have these sort of initial startup costs, the same thing applies to all decisions you make that are going to end up becoming recurring monthly expenses. You must religiously stick to the philosophy that I test my ideas with ultra low overhead. Here's what happens when you are imagine a business and it takes six to 12 months to really flesh out your idea to make your pivots and to essentially go forward and build it into a business. I'll give you a perfect example. Frank Kern and I, right? Frank's a brilliant guy, best marketer out there. I'm the best salesman out there. We decided we were going to business together, right? Many, many years ago and we figured the idea was so great because, hey, how could we fail? If Frank is doing all the marketing and I'm doing the sales, what could possibly go wrong? What could possibly go wrong? Guess what? So we started up and said, you know, this is going to be great because how could we get a big office? We'll do that. We'll hire people. We'll do all this stuff and there's simply no way it can go wrong because we're so smart. Well, we built up a huge overhead. Okay, so every single month we have this big stack of bills and guess what? Like all businesses, it's not, even with us, it wasn't the first iteration of our idea that was the right one. And we built up to get like a 50, 60, 70 thousand dollar a month overhead and we're both big earners. We could have afforded to keep running it. One day, we get the two of us, we look at each other and say, this is f**k this s**t and we closed it down. It wasn't worth it because there's just too many expenses. If you test your idea with high overhead, time is not on your side. Time is working against you. When you test your ideas with low overhead, hey, as long as it takes, you're not burning cash. There's nothing more demoralizing for an entrepreneur that when you're trying to figure the s**t out and money is flying out the window. With this business that I started here, those first three or four months when I had like 15 people on the platform, right, 30 and I'm just losing money and I'm just giving, I'm giving my stuff away for free, it seemed like, right. But I had no expenses, none. Like, I was none. My son was like my right hand man, okay, and on the pay, he's my son, right. Well, working out of the house, you know, my expenses, my bookkeepers will be for years. My bookkeepers will go without paying for months to make things work. There's everyone believed in the vision. So I had the vision and they all, we had this whole ultra low overhead environment and lo and behold, things start to work. Time is your ally. Time can be your ally as long as you keep your overhead down. As soon as you build too much overhead, time is your f*****g mortal enemy because every month you're like, oh, you don't want to even look at the mail because the f*****g bill's a born in. So you must test your ideas a low overhead. Number three, focus on situations that create positive cash flow. Positive cash is king, right. In other words, very often there are different ways, let's say you're in the product business, you have an actual physical product, right. And you have to deliver that product. You can get the product and have it manufactured in China for a unit cost. Let me flip the page. I'll show you. You can, and I'm just making up an arbitrary example, you can have that manufactured in China for 10 bucks, right, $10 or you can have it made locally for $25. And the price you're selling gets $30. So you say, well, how the f*****g can I make money at $25? But at the time I'm paying my bills, I'm going to lose money. Let's say my expenses, I'll lose, you know, $5, $10 and everything I sell. But with the China deal, f*****g, I can make a lot of money, right. The problem is, okay, is that with China, they have a nine, a three month lead time and a minimum order of a million units. You got to buy one million of these things or 100,000, let's say 100,000. So it's going to cost you a million dollars in cash, right. And you have to put that out three months ahead of the, in front, it's got to come over on the slow boat from China, right. Versus I can have a local factory do just in time inventory and replenish my goods every other day and I can fill my orders as I need them. So I'm not making any money, but I'm able to test my idea with positive cash flow. And a situation where I can, well, maybe even better still, the local factory will give me seven days of credit or 14 days. They'll give me the stuff on consignment, let me sell it to my customers, collect the money and pay them a week later. But I don't care if I am making money in the beginning. I'm just trying to prove out a concept. I know that if I can prove this is a market for my product and that I can scale it, I'll figure out all the economies of scale. How do I source it more cheaply? How do I bring down my cost of goods, my sales and my cost of goods sold? I focus on cash flow. I will, for instance, structure my, if I have a pricing, if I'm going to price things, I'll structure my prices in a way to improve cash, case in point on this business. You could sign up for 9.95 a month this program or 4.95 daily motivations or you could pay once a year and save 25%. Now, why would I do, why would I let people pay less? So essentially it's costing me money to give someone the once upfront, but it gives me more cash flow upfront to grow my business. I'll accept that. I need cash. I need to address, I need to grow. You get it now at a certain point. Maybe I'll change that. Maybe I'll know, maybe when I get to a point like on Netflix, I won't need to do that anymore because I rather just make the full 14.95 versus give the discount. But right now in this phase of my business, cash is king. Cash flow is king. So that's going to give them how I price. Also, what terms am I giving to my people to pay? I do a seven day free trial. Well, that's after eight seven days. I could, if I could not afford that I would come to a three day free trial or do no free trial at all. The point is I'm thinking that okay, I am, I could afford a seven day free trial, but then I have other products to help me manage the cash flow. I'm always thinking in terms of positive cash flow, not earnings. Don't worry about your earnings in the beginning. Worry about your cash flow and proving out your concept. That's the most important thing. Let's go to the next one. Hold on. Make sure you go in order here. Next. This is an interesting one. It's, it's the philosophy of it's, I call it don't quit your job. Meaning that, and don't get me wrong. I don't believe in dabblers. So I hate what I'm doing. This I'm doing that. I'm doing eight things and you hope one hits that very seldom works. The idea is that many of you have responsibilities in this world and there's things that, you know, you simply can't afford to roll the dice and bet the fucking farm on an idea that's unproven. So very often you have the ability as an entrepreneur to launch it, to launch your business and still maintain your current source of income. Case in point as a real entrepreneur, as a civil entrepreneur is that if you have a model that's working and you then want to essentially test a new idea, don't just say, oh, my idea is so fucking great. It's going to supplant the old one. And you have before you test the idea, you throw the old one. That's the equivalent of quitting what you're doing now. Don't throw out what you're currently doing. If it's working, do both work at night. Work hard, figure out a way to partition your time. Test your idea without quitting what you're doing now. Wait to see some traction then at a certain point then you throw the old one out and in with the new. Okay, just be careful with that because I don't want to tell you just like, you know, rocks. I'm going to say, oh, just burn your bridges, you know, and I believe in that to some level, but in doing it intelligently is the key. Okay. And then lastly, and of course, you know, this is my overall mantra for all things business and success is number five, use a proven system. Meeting model, model, model, and use mentors. If you can't find a model, model, model, don't try to reinvent the wheel. Only idiots do that. Please, whenever possible, try to find what someone else is doing similar to you, use their system, start with that as a basis and test from there. I promise you that if you do that, you tip the odds in your favor so dramatically, it's often the difference between success and failure in a new business adventure because what you're doing is starting off with something that's already working. This is why people will you buy franchises? Seriously, it's already a proven system. So whatever, but I don't, I'm not a big believer in a franchise, but I dense them, but it's not never my sweet spot. The point is, is model, model, model. Those are your rules for failing elegantly. Okay. Now, the flip side of the equation is what are you doing? The shit is right. What do you do when you have this idea that just works better than you feel like this is? How do you then scale? How do you fucking go for it? Different set of rules. In fact, that's another hour or so just to start it. So I'm going to start now.
